EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lasting winter storm brought blizzard conditions to much of southeast Idaho beginning the night of the 28th and peaking on December 29th and drawing to a close on the 30th.  The worst conditions occurred on the 29th and many major interstates closed due to the blizzard conditions.  Interstate 86 was closed from Pocatello to the interstate 84 interchange. Some details from the storm including snow totals were 13 inches at Galena Summit, 8 inches at Minidoka with 4 foot drifts, 12 inches at Driggs and 18 inches at Palisades with 4 feet drifts, 16 inches at Holbrook, 8 inches at Malad with 2 foot drifts, 12 inches at Kilgore with 3 foot drifts, 50 to 60 mph wind gusts in Pocatello, 12 to 14 inches at Hailey, 18 to 20 inches at Ketchum, over 300 road slideoffs and 60 crashes reported by the Idaho State Police, 12 inches at Sublett with whiteout conditions on the 29th, blizzard conditions on highway 91 at Swan Lake on the 29th, 9 inches at Swan Valley, 10 inches at Shoshone, 6 inches at McCammon with 2 foot drifts, 6 inches on the east benches of Pocatello, 6 inches at Burley with 3 foot drifts, 24 inches at Ashton, 10 inches at Bone, 12 inches at Springfield, 7 inches at Malta, 12 inches at Stone, 1 to 3 feet in Buist, 2 feet in Spencer, 18 inches at Island Park, 10 inches at Richfield, and 8 inches at Henry.  In addition to the interstate closures, several state highways and local roadways were closed during the day on the 29th.

A prodigious winter storm affected nearly all of southeastern Idaho beginning Christmas evening and continuing until the morning of the 27th.  Generally 1 to 2 feet of snow were common across the Snake River Plain and southeastern Highlands with the storm.  In Pocatello, it set the one day December record for snowfall with 13 inches on the 26th.  The storm totaled 14.9 inches at the NWS office at the airport.  High winds accompanied the storms with blizzard conditions in Pocatello during the evening and overnight on the 26th.  Other specific snow amounts from the storm were 20 inches on the east benches of Pocatello with 4 feet drifts.  18 inches 8 miles east of Fort Hall, 1 foot in American Falls, 1 foot in the Idaho Falls area, 15 inches in Bone, 9 inches in Grace, 16 inches at Howell Canyon, 16 inches at Wild Horse Divide, 9 inches at Soda Springs, and 15 inches at Driggs.  The Pocatello airport shut down on the 26th and although not receiving heavy snow, blizzard conditions were reported in Burley on the morning of the 26th with whiteout conditions reported on interstate 84.

A major winter storm passed across Eastern Oregon and Southwestern Idaho Sunday and Monday December 28th and 29th. This system brought with it copious amounts of snow to the region. Snow fall amounts ranged from a few inches in the lower valleys to nearly a foot near Sumpter in  Baker County Oregon and  nearly 2 feet near Pine in the central mountains of Idaho. From the morning of December 12th through the morning of December 14th a winter storm dumped heavy snow across the Idaho panhandle. In the Northern Panhandle Mountains, Schweitzer Mountain received 12 inches of new snow. Seven inches fell at priest River, and 6 inches was reported at Sandpoint. A spotter reported 6 to 10 inches around Naples and 3 to 4 inches fell near Porthill. In the Coeur D'Alene Area a spotter at Rathdrum reported 12 inches of snow and Spirit Lake reported 4 inches. In the Central Panhandle Mountains Lookout Pass reported 30 inches of new snow, Silver Mountain received 16 inches and the town of Boville reported 4 inches. On the Camas Prairie, Winchester reported 6 inches of new snow.

During the night of November 20th a slow moving winter storm dumped heavy snow over a relatively narrow band in the Idaho panhandle. During the morning of November 19th a low level jet stream was detected by weather balloons over extreme eastern Washington and northern Idaho. At daybreak these strong winds aloft started to descend to the surface producing scattered damage from downed trees. In the northern Idaho panhandle a dozen large pine trees fell on summer homes along Lake Pend Oreille. In the Coeur D'Alene area numerous trees fell on houses and power lines. By 10:00 am a cold front swept through the region ending the strong southwesterly winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a night of new snowfall and warming daytime temperatures, two separate avalanches occurred just an hour and a half apart and within 20 miles of each other. The first event took place around 11:30 am near Keokee Lake about 10 miles northwest of Schweitzer Ski Resort.  A couple of back country skiers were caught in an avalanche, and one was buried. The second event took place around 1 pm about 5 miles east of Priest Lake. A group of snowmobilers were caught in an avalanche, and one was buried.

Southwestwinds of 20 to 35 mph caused blowing and drifting of snow and reduced visibility.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 3 inches of heavy rain caused a cottonwood tree to fall and crush a Ford Pickup truck and cause a small fire. Power was also out for 4 hours for about 100 residents. The rain also caused minor flooding  in the Community Park and the mobile park both in Troy and Bovill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rain and snowmelt led to flooding on both the smaller and major rivers across eastern Kootenai, Shoshone and much of Latah counties.  The flood stage on the Couer d'Alene river at Cataldo peaked at 43.7 ft with flood stage of 43 feet. This caused flooding around a home near Wolf Creek Lodge and road closures on Riverview Drive.

A long duration snow event brought heavy snow to north central Idaho from March 5 through March 8. A series of Pacific storm systems dropped enough snow to be measured in feet rather than inches across the region. Total accumulations ranged from 1-3 feet across the Clearwater Mountain Region with the extremes of 41 inches at Dixie and 42 inches at Pierce, and 1-2 feet in the mountains of Lemhi County. Power outages and schools were closed in Clearwater County on March 7 due to heavy snow and downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful pacific storm with a very strong jet stream moved over north central Idaho during the late night and early morning hours, producing strong damaging winds. Wind gusts to 58 mph blew over a camp trailer with people inside near Riggins, ID with no injuries reported. In Lemhi County, wind gusts between 60 to 80 mph blew over trees, power lines and started fires. Trees were blown down on South St. Charles Street and U.S. Highway 93 was covered with rocks and tree branches between Salmon and North Fork. A deck was blown off a trailer and damaged a truck, and a dog was killed by a fallen tree on Cherokee Lane. A power pole fell and caught fire on U.S. Highway 93, 2.2 miles south of Salmon, with minor roof damage also reported in the area.

During the evening of October 28th, a strong cold front caused high winds over portions of the Idaho panhandle. In Nez Perce county a remote weather station recorded a wind gust of 63 mph. A storm spotter reported winds gusting to 51 mph at Winchester in Lewis County. In Moscow, ID a chip truck was blown over and a falling tree crushed a car. At Calder in Shoshone county, the terrain channeled the wind to an estimated 65 mph sustained speed. A remote weather station at Lines Creek recorded a gust of 64 mph..

Two fire fighters were killed on the Cramer Fire, 17 miles west of Shoup in western Lemhi County. Several days of hot and dry conditions caused the Cramer Fire to expand rapidly. The fire fighters were killed near a helipad waiting for a helicopter to pick them up, when the fire over took them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and dry weather covered all of southeast Idaho during the month of July.  It was the 8th warmest July ever recorded at the Pocatello airport and the average high temperature for the month of 94.6 degrees was the warmest ever for the city.  Pocatello only received a trace of rain for the month which also made it the driest July on record.  Pocatello surpassed 100 degrees on 9 days including 6 out of 7 days from July 18th through July 24th.  The longest streak was 5 days in a row from July 20th through the 24th.

2600 acre Festival fire northeast of Blackfoot.  Burned short grass and scattered brush and juniper.  No damage reported.
4100 acre Timmerman fire north of Shoshone burned grass and sagebrush.  No property damage reported.
1500 acre Foothills fire south of Picabo.  Burned grass ans sagebrush.  No property damage reported.
1300 acre Crystal Creek fire northwest of Stanley burned sub-alpine fir, brush and grass.  No structural damage.  1.5 million dollars to suppress.  contained by August 5th.
Flash flooding in Rockland.  Bridges washed out in the Stewart Canyon.
Flash flooding in Juniper.  Heavy water on interstate 15.  18 inches deep and 100 feet wide.  Running water on interstate.

4500 acre Harkness wildfire started started by a transient's campfire.  Several buildings destroyed by fire including 2 homes and 8 additional barns or outbuildings and several vehicles and farm equipment also destroyed.  Entire Harkness Canyon area evacuated.  1900 homes in Arimo, Lava Hot Springs, Lund and McCammon lost power.  US route 30 from McCammon to Lava Hot Springs was closed as fire as fire burned along the route during afternoon and evening of July 21st.
1500 acre Crystal Creek wildfire 18 miles north/northwest of Stanley.  Burned sub-alpine fur, brush and grass.  Cost to suppress 1.5 million dollars.  No property damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and dry weather covered all of southeast Idaho during the month of July.  It was the 8th warmest July ever recorded at the Pocatello airport and the average high temperature for the month of 94.6 degrees was the warmest ever for the city.  Pocatello only received a trace of rain for the month which also made it the driest July on record.  Pocatello surpassed 100 degrees on 9 days including 6 out of 7 days from July 18th through July 24th.  The longest streak was 5 days in a row from July 20th through the 24th.
Thunderstorm wind uprooted tree and downed phone lines in town of Basalt.  No property damage reported.
2300 acre Blackrock wildfire started by two teenagers burned sage and grass near Inkom southeast of Pocatello.  No structural damage.
